section.title: The Righteous Branch and False Prophets
section.sectionRationale: Having exposed Judah's failed kings, Jeremiah now names the failed shepherds outright and answers their failure with the book's clearest messianic promise, a righteous Branch called 'The LORD Our Righteousness'. The chapter then turns to expose the false prophets who spoke comforting lies in God's name, pressing the urgent question of how God's true word can be told from human invention.
section.thesis: Where Judah's shepherd-kings scattered the flock, God Himself promises to raise up a righteous Branch who will reign in true justice and be called The LORD Our Righteousness, and He exposes the false prophets whose comfortable lies stand in direct contrast to His fiery, hammering word.
section.frameNote: Three movements: judgment on the scattering shepherds and the promise of the righteous Branch, an extended exposure of false prophets who speak dreams instead of God's word, and a closing rebuke of those who trade in the 'burden of the LORD' as a slogan.

verses.text: “Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of My pasture!” declares the LORD.

Therefore this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says about the shepherds who tend My people: “You have scattered My flock and driven them away, and have not attended to them. Behold, I will attend to you for the evil of your deeds, declares the LORD.
Then I Myself will gather the remnant of My flock from all the lands to which I have banished them, and I will return them to their pasture, where they will be fruitful and multiply.
I will raise up shepherds over them who will tend them, and they will no longer be afraid or dismayed, nor will any go missing, declares the LORD.

Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will raise up for David a righteous {{tsemach|Branch}}, and He will reign wisely as King and will administer justice and righteousness in the land.
In His days Judah will be saved, and Israel will dwell securely. And this is His name by which He will be called: {{yhwhtsidqenu|The LORD Our Righteousness}}.
So behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when they will no longer say, ‘As surely as the LORD lives, who brought the Israelites up out of the land of Egypt.’

Instead they will say, ‘As surely as the LORD lives, who brought and led the descendants of the house of Israel up out of the land of the north and all the other lands to which He had banished them.’ Then they will dwell once more in their own land.”

units.label: Against the false prophets
units.note: dreams instead of the true word

verses.text:
As for the prophets: 
My heart is broken within me, and all my bones tremble. I have become like a drunkard, like a man overcome by wine, because of the LORD, because of His holy words.

For the land is full of adulterers, because of the curse, the land mourns 
and the pastures of the wilderness have dried up, their course is evil and their power is misused.
“For both prophet and priest are ungodly; even in My house I have found their wickedness,” declares the LORD.
“Therefore their path will become slick; they will be driven away into the darkness and fall into it. For I will bring disaster upon them in the year of their punishment,” declares the LORD.
“Among the prophets of Samaria I saw an offensive thing: They prophesied by Baal and led My people Israel astray.

And among the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen a horrible thing: They commit adultery and walk in lies. They strengthen the hands of evildoers, so that no one turns his back on wickedness. They are all like Sodom to Me; the people of Jerusalem are like Gomorrah.”

Therefore this is what the LORD of Hosts says concerning the prophets: 
“I will feed them wormwood and give them poisoned water to drink, for from the prophets of Jerusalem ungodliness has spread throughout the land.”

This is what the LORD of Hosts says: 
“Do not listen to the words of the prophets who prophesy to you. They are filling you with false hopes. They speak visions from their own minds, not from the mouth of the LORD.
They keep saying to those who despise Me, ‘The LORD says that you will have peace,’ and to everyone who walks in the stubbornness of his own heart, ‘No harm will come to you.’
But which of them has stood in the council of the LORD to see and hear His word? Who has given heed to His word and obeyed it?
Behold, the storm of the LORD has gone out with fury, a whirlwind swirling down upon the heads of the wicked.
The anger of the LORD will not turn back until He has fully accomplished the purposes of His heart. In the days to come you will understand this clearly.
I did not send these prophets, yet they have run with their message; I did not speak to them, yet they have prophesied.
But if they had stood in My council, they would have proclaimed My words to My people and turned them back from their evil ways and deeds.”
“Am I only a God nearby,” declares the LORD, “and not a God far away?”

“Can a man hide in secret places where I cannot see him?” declares the LORD. 
“Do I not fill the heavens and the earth?” declares the LORD.
“I have heard the sayings of the prophets who prophesy lies in My name: ‘I had a dream! I had a dream!’
How long will this continue in the hearts of these prophets who prophesy falsehood, these prophets of the delusion of their own minds?
They suppose the dreams that they tell one another will make My people forget My name, just as their fathers forgot My name through the worship of Baal.
Let the prophet who has a dream retell it, but let him who has My word speak it truthfully. For what is straw compared to grain?” declares the LORD.
“Is not My word like fire,” declares the LORD, “and like a hammer that smashes a rock?”
“Therefore behold,” declares the LORD, “I am against the prophets who steal from one another words they attribute to Me.”
“Yes,” declares the LORD, “I am against the prophets who wag their own tongues and proclaim, ‘The LORD declares it.’”

“Indeed,” declares the LORD, “I am against those who prophesy false dreams and retell them to lead My people astray with their reckless lies. It was not I who sent them or commanded them, and they are of no benefit at all to these people,” declares the LORD.

units.label: The burden of the LORD
units.note: a slogan turned back on those who misuse it

verses.text: “Now when this people or a prophet or priest asks you, ‘What is the {{massa|burden}} of the LORD?’ you are to say to them, ‘What burden? I will forsake you, declares the LORD.’
As for the prophet or priest or anyone who claims, ‘This is the burden of the LORD,’ I will punish that man and his household.
This is what each man is to say to his friend and to his brother: ‘What has the LORD answered?’ or ‘What has the LORD spoken?’
But refer no more to the burden of the LORD, for each man's word becomes the burden, so that you pervert the words of the living God, the LORD of Hosts, our God.
Thus you are to say to the prophet: ‘What has the LORD answered you?’ and ‘What has the LORD spoken?’
But if you claim, ‘This is the burden of the LORD,’ then this is what the LORD says: Because you have said, ‘This is the burden of the LORD,’ and I specifically told you not to make this claim,
therefore I will surely forget you and will cast you out of My presence, both you and the city that I gave to you and your fathers.
And I will bring upon you everlasting shame and perpetual humiliation that will never be forgotten.”

tsemach.gloss: Branch or sprout, a shoot that grows up from a root or stump. Used here as a royal title for the coming Davidic king, it pictures new life springing from what looks like a cut-off, dying dynasty, and becomes a fixed messianic title, repeated in Jeremiah 33:15, Zechariah 3:8, and 6:12.

yhwhtsidqenu.gloss: The LORD our righteousness, the name given to the coming King, wordplay directly against Zedekiah, whose own name means 'the LORD is my righteousness' but whose reign utterly failed to live it out. Unlike the failed kings, this Branch's very name declares that righteousness before God comes only through Him.

massa.gloss: A word that means both burden and oracle or utterance, the same term prophets used for a solemn prophetic pronouncement. God plays on this double meaning: when people flippantly ask for 'the burden of the LORD' as slang for the latest prophecy, He turns the word back on them, they themselves have become the burden He will cast off.

basic.context: After exposing Judah's failed kings by name, Jeremiah now names their failure directly, calling them shepherds who scattered God's flock, and answers it with the clearest promise yet of a coming King from David's line who will actually reign in justice, called by the striking name The LORD Our Righteousness. The chapter then turns to expose false prophets who told the people comfortable lies in God's name, insisting that God's true word is like fire and a hammer, not a pleasant dream, and closes by rebuking those who casually toss around 'the burden of the LORD' as religious slang.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter directly names its problem and its solution: failed shepherds versus a promised righteous Shepherd-King, and false prophets versus God's true word. The hardest interpretive question is how anyone could tell a true prophet from a false one when both claimed to speak for God; verses 21 to 22 and 28 to 29 give the honest test, true prophets actually turn people from evil and their word carries the weight and permanence of fire and a hammer, not the vague comfort of an invented dream.
basic.originalLanguages: God promises a tsemach, a Branch or sprout, springing from David's line even though it looks cut down, and this King's name will be YHWH tsidqenu, the LORD our righteousness, a deliberate echo and rebuke of King Zedekiah's own name, which meant something similar but whose life proved false to it. The false prophets are exposed through wordplay too: they ask flippantly for the massa, the burden or oracle, of the LORD, and God turns the word back on them, declaring that they themselves have become the burden He will cast away.
basic.typology: The righteous Branch springing from David's apparently ruined line anticipates Isaiah's shoot from the stump of Jesse, both fulfilled in Christ, who comes from David's royal line yet arises out of a dynasty under judgment. The contrast between comfortable false prophecy and God's fiery, hammering true word anticipates the New Testament's warnings against those who preach 'peace, peace' when there is no peace.
basic.christ: Jeremiah 23:5-6 is one of the Old Testament's clearest messianic prophecies: Christ is the righteous Branch who actually administers justice and righteousness, and He alone can bear the name The LORD Our Righteousness, since Paul teaches that believers are counted righteous only through union with Him, 1 Corinthians 1:30. Where false prophets offered cheap comfort, Christ is the true and faithful Word made flesh, John 1:14, who does not flatter sinners but calls them to real repentance.
basic.god: God is grieved, His own heart is described as broken, over corrupt shepherds and lying prophets who exploit His name for comfort rather than truth, yet He remains faithful to His promise to David even through their total failure. His word is not vague or malleable like a dream; it is as unbreakable as fire and a hammer, true regardless of how many false voices claim to speak for Him.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 1:30 calls Christ our righteousness, echoing the very title given the Branch here. Isaiah 11:1 describes a shoot from the stump of Jesse, the same royal image developed further in this chapter. 2 Peter 2:1-3 warns of false teachers who exploit people with fabricated words, exactly what Jeremiah exposes among the prophets of his day. Ezekiel 34 develops the same shepherd imagery, judging Israel's failed shepherds and promising God Himself will shepherd His flock.
basic.oneStory: The failed shepherds of Israel expose humanity's constant need for a true King and a true word from God, not comfortable lies. That need is answered in Christ, the righteous Branch called The LORD Our Righteousness, whose word, unlike every false prophet's, is as sure and unbreakable as fire and a hammer, and whose reign will finally administer the justice and righteousness this whole broken history was waiting for.

context.literary: This chapter forms the resolution to the preceding indictment of Judah's kings, moving from failed shepherds to the promised righteous Shepherd-King, before pivoting to a distinct but related oracle exposing false prophets, tied together by the same theme of failed spiritual leadership contrasted with God's true, coming word and rule.
context.historical: False prophets were a persistent problem throughout Jeremiah's ministry, offering the people reassurance of peace and quick deliverance from Babylon that directly contradicted Jeremiah's message of coming judgment, a conflict that comes to a head dramatically with Hananiah in chapter 28.
context.canonical: Jeremiah 23:5-6 stands alongside Isaiah 9:6-7 and 11:1-5 as one of the Old Testament's central messianic prophecies of a righteous Davidic King, later quoted and echoed extensively in the New Testament's presentation of Jesus as the fulfillment of David's throne.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the failure of Judah's shepherd-kings and the corruption of its prophets while anchoring hope firmly in God's own coming solution, a righteous Branch who will reign as David's true heir and be called by God's own righteousness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The oracle against the false prophets describes a specific historical crisis of competing prophetic claims; it prescribes an enduring test for discerning true from false teaching, whether the message turns people from evil, verse 22, and whether it carries the weight and consistency of God's actual word rather than human invention, verse 28.

debates.question: How could Jeremiah's original audience tell a true prophet from a false one when both claimed the LORD's authority?

views.label: The moral fruit test
views.case: Verse 22 gives a practical test: a true prophet's message turns people back from evil ways, while these false prophets, by contrast, 'strengthen the hands of evildoers,' verse 14, so their fruit exposes their falsehood.

views.label: The content and consistency test
views.case: Verses 28-29 point to the character of the message itself, God's true word is like fire and a hammer, weighty and consistent, not the vague, self-serving comfort ('you will have peace') the false prophets offered regardless of the people's actual conduct.

debates.bearing: Both tests work together: false prophecy can be identified both by its moral effect, excusing sin, and by its content, offering comfort untethered from truth, a pattern still relevant for testing any message claimed to be from God.

study.originalLanguages: God promises a tsemach, a Branch, rising from David's royal line even though that line looks as good as cut down, and gives this coming King the name YHWH tsidqenu, the LORD our righteousness, a pointed echo and rebuke of Zedekiah's own name and failed reign. The false prophets, meanwhile, are exposed through a play on the word massa, which means both burden and prophetic oracle, God declares that those who flippantly demand 'the burden of the LORD' have themselves become the burden He will cast away.

typology.type: The righteous Branch rising from David's line, Jeremiah 23:5-6
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the true Son of David who actually administers justice and righteousness and who alone can bear the name The LORD Our Righteousness, 1 Corinthians 1:30.

typology.type: God's true word as fire and a hammer against false, comforting dreams, Jeremiah 23:28-29
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the enduring, unbreakable authority of Christ's own word, contrasted throughout the New Testament with false teachers who preach comfortable but empty messages, 2 Peter 2:1-3.

study.christ: Jeremiah 23:5-6 stands as one of the Old Testament's clearest messianic prophecies: the righteous Branch who will actually reign in justice and righteousness, in contrast to every failed king before him, and whose very name, The LORD Our Righteousness, Paul later applies directly to Christ, who alone becomes our righteousness before God, 1 Corinthians 1:30. Where the false prophets offered comfortable lies detached from truth, Christ is the true and living Word, who does not flatter sinners with false peace but calls them to real repentance while offering real, lasting righteousness in Himself.
study.god: God's own heart is grieved, described as broken and trembling, over shepherds who scatter His flock and prophets who prophesy lies in His name for their own gain. Yet His covenant faithfulness to David does not waver, and His true word remains as unbreakable and enduring as fire and a hammer, regardless of how many false voices claim to speak in His name.

crossReferences.note: Paul calls Christ our righteousness, directly echoing the title given to the Branch, The LORD Our Righteousness.
Describes a shoot rising from the stump of Jesse, the same image of new royal life from an apparently dead line developed here.
Develops the same shepherd imagery, judging Israel's failed shepherds and promising that God Himself will shepherd His flock.
Warns of false teachers who exploit people with fabricated words for their own gain, exactly the pattern Jeremiah exposes among the false prophets.

study.oneStory: The failed shepherds and lying prophets of Jeremiah's day expose humanity's deep need for both a true King and a true word from God rather than comfortable invention. That need is met fully in Christ, the righteous Branch called The LORD Our Righteousness, whose word endures like fire and a hammer and whose reign finally brings the justice and righteousness this entire broken history of failed leaders had promised but never delivered.

sermon.title: I Will Raise Up for David a Righteous Branch
sermon.why: Piper works through Jeremiah 21:11 to 23:8, tracing the judgment on Judah's failed kings and showing how it resolves precisely in the promise of the righteous Branch named The LORD Our Righteousness, fulfilled in Christ, the exact movement this study traces.

quiz.q: What accusation does God bring against the shepherds in verses 1 to 2?

options.text: They have scattered and destroyed God's flock instead of attending to them.
options.note: The 'shepherds' are Judah's kings, judged for neglecting and scattering God's people.

options.text: They cared for the flock too gently and generously.
options.note: The charge is neglect and destruction, the opposite of excessive gentleness.

options.text: They failed to build enough grand buildings.
options.note: The accusation concerns care for people, not construction projects.

options.text: They were too focused on foreign military alliances.
options.note: The specific charge is scattering and not attending to the flock, not foreign policy as such.

quiz.q: What is significant about the coming King's name, 'The LORD Our Righteousness'?

options.text: It directly contrasts with Zedekiah's own name and failed reign, showing true righteousness comes only through this King.
options.note: Zedekiah's name carried a similar meaning that his life utterly failed to embody; the Branch's name declares the reality Zedekiah's did not.

options.text: It has no connection to any other name or king in the passage.
options.note: The wordplay with Zedekiah's name is a deliberate and pointed contrast in the passage's context.

options.text: It means the King will be righteous but will not help His people become righteous.
options.note: The name declares that righteousness for God's people comes through this King, not that it is withheld from them.

options.text: It is simply a poetic title with no theological content.
options.note: The name carries substantial theological weight, later applied directly to Christ, 1 Corinthians 1:30.

quiz.q: According to verse 22, what is the true test of a genuine prophet?

options.text: A true prophet, having stood in God's council, would turn people back from their evil ways and deeds.
options.note: This is the explicit test given: true prophecy actually produces repentance, not comfortable excuse.

options.text: A true prophet always tells people exactly what they want to hear.
options.note: This describes the false prophets condemned in the passage, not the true test.

options.text: A true prophet must perform visible miracles to prove authenticity.
options.note: The test given here is about moral fruit and turning people from evil, not miraculous signs.

options.text: A true prophet never predicts any coming judgment.
options.note: Jeremiah himself, a true prophet, predicts extensive judgment throughout the book.

quiz.q: What does God mean by comparing His word to fire and a hammer in verse 29?

options.text: God's true word carries real, unbreakable weight and power, unlike the vague, invented dreams of false prophets.
options.note: The contrast is directly with 'dreams' in the preceding verses, straw compared to grain, fire and a hammer compared to invented visions.

options.text: It means God's word is meant to be destructive without any purpose.
options.note: The image emphasizes the word's power and permanence, contrasted with weightless false prophecy, not destruction for its own sake.

options.text: It refers to a literal fire that consumed the temple.
options.note: This is figurative language about the nature and authority of God's word, not a description of a literal event.

options.text: It shows that God's word changes constantly like fire.
options.note: The image emphasizes consistency and unbreakable force, the opposite of changeability.

quiz.q: Why does God rebuke the casual use of the phrase 'the burden of the LORD' in verses 33 to 38?

options.text: The people had turned a solemn prophetic term into flippant slang, so God declares that they themselves have become the burden He will cast off.
options.note: The wordplay on massa, meaning both burden and oracle, is turned back on those who misused it.

options.text: God approved of the phrase and encouraged its continued use.
options.note: God explicitly forbids the continued use of this phrase in verse 36.

options.text: The phrase referred to a physical burden of taxation, unrelated to prophecy.
options.note: The passage is specifically about misuse of a prophetic term, not taxation.

options.text: It was a minor stylistic issue with no real consequence.
options.note: The consequence is severe: those who persist in the phrase face being cast out of God's presence.

questions: The false prophets told people what they wanted to hear rather than what was true. Where are you tempted to seek out comfortable words rather than God's actual truth?
The true test of a prophet was whether the message turned people from evil, verse 22. Does the teaching or content you consume actually call you toward repentance, or only toward comfort?

The righteous Branch is named 'The LORD Our Righteousness', our righteousness comes from Him, not from ourselves. Where are you still trying to establish your own righteousness rather than receiving His?
God's word is like fire and a hammer, unbreakable and weighty. How does that shape the way you receive Scripture when it confronts rather than comforts you?
The people had turned reverent language into casual slogans, 'the burden of the LORD'. Is there language about God or His word that you use carelessly rather than with the weight it deserves?

translationNotes.note: 'Righteous Branch', tsemach, becomes a fixed messianic title, repeated in Jeremiah 33:15 and developed further in Zechariah 3:8 and 6:12, always describing new royal life springing from David's apparently ruined line.

'The LORD Our Righteousness', YHWH tsidqenu, deliberately echoes and contrasts with the name Zedekiah, which carries a similar meaning; the King's name declares what the failed king's name only promised.

'Burden', massa, is the same Hebrew word regularly used elsewhere for a solemn prophetic 'oracle' or 'utterance'; the wordplay is largely lost in English translation, God turns the people's flippant slang back on them as a genuine, weighty pronouncement of judgment.
